comments.post_body:
We've given away over 6,000 books in the last 3 weeks. 

A couple weeks ago, I posted here on LinkedIn about a book we've been giving out at SF AI meetups.

It's called Principles of Building AI Agents, and it's become popular among developers starting to build with AI, as well as their PMs and CEOs.

One engineer said that he started reading after a person he didn't know gave him one on a bus. 

A current YC founder called it "the most popular book in SF right now."

I expected to get a few dozen commenters. Instead, over 3,000 people have dropped us a line.

My LinkedIn DMs went crazy, and our team's been sending out copy after copy.

And if you want one, just comment "BOOK" and we'll send it over!
comments.context_summary: The author is giving away a popular book called Principles of Building AI Agents, with over 6,000 copies given away in the last 3 weeks, and is offering to send a copy to anyone who comments 'BOOK' on the post. The book has gained significant interest among developers, product managers, and CEOs, with many reaching out to the author to get a copy.

comments.comment_body: Awesome - now looking forward for an invite to host the NAM team.

comments.post_body:
"My Precious!"

When my 13-year old's school vacation came around a couple of months ago, I did what every self-respecting helicoptering parent would do. I decided unilaterally that the best use of his time is to ensure that he does not suffer from the 2024 Oxford word of the year ("Brain rot"). That of course meant finding time away from contributing to upping the stock price of my company to focusing on my investment (my child) to increase my returns over time.

My son is an avid reader which I take the full genetic credit for. However, this time I chose to change the game and challenged him to attempt a different task to pass his time. We had a 500-piece jigsaw puzzle of Gollum, the puny creature who took it upon himself to keep Sauron single-eyed for nearly five hundred years.

With great enthusiasm comes a great start and a great one it was. He and I attacked the puzzle with gusto and with daily persistence got the outer boundaries of the puzzle going in a few days. The sessions were not drab. There was the Caravan Sa Re Ga Ma device playing old Hindi film songs as we powered through. And then followed the plateau. The pieces were hard to find. Everything looked like everything else. The colors and shades seemed to all blend into a hypnotic mess leading to headaches, fatigue and a general desire to leave it as is.

Just like in Lord of the rings, the days turned into weeks and the weeks into months. And Gollum lay there, quietly biding his time, mocking us with his smile since we had already put his face together. Flames of war were being lit around the puzzle. Our house help complained that the table could not be dusted because of the puzzle, our guests had to be given teacups directly in their hands since the puzzle dominated the table. 

Time became my enemy. I was Captain Ahab and the puzzle my whale. I chased it on weekends, five to ten pieces at a time. Strategies were employed to find similar pieces, I sought the help of my wife and son (who had long since moved on to brain rotting activities), Bangalore dust was repeatedly wiped off the pieces and the final pieces put together!

What did I take away from this exercise that makes it a good LinkedIn post? That persistence matters? That strategy matters? That you lead by example? All of the above, but the biggest takeaway was the one I should have known going into this enterprise - I need to get my eyesight checked!

#FridayMusings #Humour
comments.context_summary: A parent shares their experience of working on a 500-piece jigsaw puzzle of Gollum with their 13-year-old son during school vacation, describing the challenges and frustrations they faced in completing it. The commenter is responding to the parent's humorous and relatable account of their puzzle adventure.

comments.comment_body:
Congratulations Mathangi. There’s only upside for Zuora now on. I’m sure there will be more subscribers to Zuora Product updates. 
All the best
comments.post_body: Excited to share that I joined the #zuora family as Vice President of Product Management. Looking forward to enjoying my journey as a ZEO. I loved every moment of being a Microsoftie and extremely thankful to all my colleagues, mentors and leaders that continue to inspire me #thankyou #microsoft. It was not easy to make the decision, but my joy doubles as I look forward to bring to customers the integrated power of #zuora  and #microsoft products as well. Learning every day and always inspired by people around me!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is congratulating the author on their new role as Vice President of Product Management at Zuora, after leaving Microsoft, and is likely expressing excitement about the potential integration of Zuora and Microsoft products. The author is looking forward to their new journey and is grateful for their past experiences and relationships at Microsoft.

comments.context_summary: The commenter is congratulating Mathangi on her new role as Vice President of Product Management at Zuora, expressing confidence in the company's future success with her on board. The original post announced Mathangi's departure from Microsoft to join Zuora, where she looks forward to integrating Zuora and Microsoft products.
